turns.content: I'm building a generic configuration system and I've been using serde_json::Value to define the shape of my config structs. Right now I'm trying to make the max_depth field const in a trait, but when I try to implement it on a struct that holds a String, the compiler says the type isn't known at compile time because the value needs to be resolved later. Is using serde_json::Value with associated constants even possible?

The issue is that you cannot use const generics or const parameters for types like String because those are not known at compile time, only the constant value itself can be const. If your trait requires a specific type parameter to compute an associated constant, you need to make sure that type parameter is fixed when the trait is implemented. You might consider using const generics with integer bounds instead of storing a type in the config, or use a separate crate like serde_json::Value for runtime configuration but keep the structural constraints in your types as static constants.

Ah okay so I can't just declare max_depth as an associated const that depends on the String type. But wait, if I make my trait generic over T and then implement it for String, won't that solve the problem? Like Trait<T> where T: SomeBound, then impl Trait<String>, and inside I define const MAX_DEPTH = 10; wouldn't that work?

Yes, that is exactly how you do it. You define the trait with a type parameter T, then implement that trait for your specific type like String or serde_json::Value, and within that implementation block you can declare const MAX_DEPTH = 10; as an associated constant. The compiler knows T at the point of implementation even if it was generic before, so the const is valid once the impl block is processed.

That makes sense now. So I should define my Trait<T> with associated constants inside it, then just write impl MyTrait<String> for String and put the actual values there? But what if later I want to support serde_json::Value too?

You would need separate implementation blocks. One block is impl MyTrait<String> where you define const MAX_DEPTH = 10; and another block is impl MyTrait<serde_json::Value> where you might set it to something like 20 or use a different rule. The trait definition itself stays generic with the associated constants as placeholders that get filled in by each specific implementation.
Got it, so I can't have one const value for both types if they need different behaviors. But what about const generics? Can I use those to limit the depth instead of hardcoding numbers?

Const generics are useful when you want a fixed size parameter at compile time, like Vec<usize> with a fixed length. For your config system where different types might need different constants, associated constants defined inside trait implementations are the right approach. Const generics won't help here because you're not trying to constrain a type size but rather define static values per-type.
